title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who took up beekeeping in the city. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Julian, The Rooftop Enthusiast
'I wanted to contribute to the local ecosystem,' Julian says. [51]The hardest part was the physical strain of moving heavy honey supers down several flights of stairs when the lift broke.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my general anxiety levels dropped significantly; there's something hypnotic about the hum of the hive.[/48] [53]I learned that bees are a mirror of the environment; if the city is stressed, the colony reflects it.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in software architecture helped me design a more efficient layout for the hive components.[/50] [47]Initially, the sheer heat of wearing a full protective suit in mid-July was almost unbearable.[/47] It was a sticky but rewarding lesson.
texts.B:
### Sarah, The Community Garden Keeper
Sarah manages hives in a shared neighborhood space. [49]The experience taught me that we are far more dependent on these tiny insects for our food security than the average person realizes.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the deep sense of responsibility I’d feel for the survival of the queen during a harsh winter.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how much the noise of the city seemed to agitate the bees during the first few weeks.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the local community's curiosity, which turned every hive inspection into an impromptu educational seminar.[/54] [56]If I hadn't attended a talk at the local library, I never would have considered starting.[/56] It was a sweet success.
texts.C:
### Victor, The Balcony Beginner
Victor kept a small hive on his apartment balcony. [51]I struggled with the legalities; navigating the complex local bylaws regarding urban livestock was more taxing than the bees themselves.[/51] [48]I noticed that my observational skills became much sharper, as I started noticing every flowering plant in a three-mile radius.[/48] [53]I realized that honey is effectively a liquid map of the local neighborhood's floral diversity.[/53] [50]I found that the precision I used in my previous career as a watchmaker was perfect for the delicate task of queen rearing.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the constant sticky residue on everything I owned, which I found quite irritating initially.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried my neighbors would complain.
texts.D:
### Elena, The School Project Mentor
Elena helped set up an observation hive at a primary school. [49]The project made me realize that education is the most powerful tool we have for environmental conservation.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the opportunity if my former biology teacher hadn't reached out for technical advice.[/56] [54]I was moved by the fearlessness of the children, who were far more interested than intimidated by the bees.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the mental fatigue of constant vigilance required when children are around live hives.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of explaining complex pheromone communication to seven-year-olds incredibly challenging.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned about potential allergies, but the safety protocols were flawless.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or sensory impact of the urban environment on themselves or the bees?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions the heat of the suit, Sarah (B) the city noise agitating bees, and Elena (D) the mental fatigue of vigilance.

questions.question: notes a positive change in their mental well-being or observational habits?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) saw his anxiety drop, and Victor (C) found his observational skills became sharper.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on food dependency or the power of education?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on our dependency on bees for food, and Elena (D) on education as a conservation tool.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous to beekeeping?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) used software architecture skills, and Victor (C) used watchmaking precision.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to physical logistics or legal regulations?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) struggled with heavy lifting/broken lift, and Victor (C) with local bylaws.

questions.question: admits to having had prior concerns or having developed an unexpected sense of duty?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt a deep responsibility for the queen, and Victor (C) worried about neighbor complaints.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about the environment or the nature of honey?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) saw bees as a mirror of the environment, and Victor (C) saw honey as a liquid map.

questions.question: highlights the value of the public's interest or the attitude of a specific group?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued the community's curiosity, and Elena (D) was moved by the children's fearlessness.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the less pleasant or complex aspects of the task?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the sticky residue, and Elena (D) found explaining pheromones to kids challenging.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external trigger or contact that led to the project?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) attended a library talk, and Elena (D) was contacted by a former teacher.
